---
title: "M1 — SPI Bayesian AR(1) Forecasting"
subtitle: "Drought Modeling and Probabilistic Forecasting in the Chélif Basin (Algeria)"
author: |
  Imene DERRAR  
  Supervisor: Dr. Abdelillah OTMANE CHERIF  
  NHSM (National Higher School of Mathematics) · IHFR (Institut Hydrométéorologique de Formation et de Recherche)  
  Final-year engineering thesis in Applied Mathematics — Specialization: Statistics, Econometrics and Actuarial Science — Academic year 2025–2026
date: ""
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    toc_float: true
    code_folding: show
    df_print: paged
    theme: readable
    highlight: tango
  pdf_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    number_sections: true
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: console
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo      = TRUE,
  message   = FALSE,
  warning   = FALSE,
  fig.width = 9,
  fig.height = 4.5,
  fig.align = "center",
  dpi       = 110,
  cache     = FALSE
)
```

# Chapter scope

This chapter develops a unified classical-plus-Bayesian framework for modeling
and probabilistically forecasting the regional **Standardized Precipitation
Index at the 6-month accumulation scale ($\mathrm{SPI}_{6}$)** over the Chélif
basin (Algeria). The sequence implemented below mirrors the mathematical and
operational logic of the thesis pipeline:

1. Experimental setup (paths, seed, hyper-parameters)
2. Data loading and train/test split
3. Stochastic characterization (descriptive, ACF, PACF, rolling moments)
4. Stationarity testing (ADF + KPSS, joint reading)
5. SARIMA: controlled grid, selection, residual diagnostics
6. Bayesian AR(1) with conjugate Normal–Inverse-Gamma prior (closed form)
7. Rolling-origin out-of-sample forecasting
8. Evaluation metrics: RMSE, MAE, CRPS (`scoringRules`), coverage, sharpness
9. Diebold–Mariano pairwise comparison (Newey-West HAC)
10. Validation / sanity checks
11. Drought exceedance probabilities $\mathbb{P}(\mathrm{SPI}\le c)$
12. Reliability / calibration diagram
13. Final synthesis
14. Export of outputs


---

# 1. Experimental setup

```{r packages}
# ---- 1.1  Packages -------------------------------------------------------
# Each package is included for a specific function:
#   tidyverse / lubridate : data wrangling, dates, plotting (ggplot2)
#   zoo, xts              : irregular and rolling time-series operations
#   forecast              : ARIMA, SARIMA, automatic order search, accuracy
#   tseries               : adf.test, kpss.test, jarque.bera.test
#   urca                  : richer ADF / KPSS formulations (Said-Dickey, etc.)
#   scoringRules          : proper scoring rules (CRPS, log-score, etc.)
#   patchwork             : compose ggplot panels
#   knitr / kableExtra    : presentable tables in the rendered notebook

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  pkgs <- c("tidyverse", "lubridate", "zoo", "xts",
            "forecast", "tseries", "urca", "scoringRules",
            "patchwork", "knitr", "kableExtra")
  for (p in pkgs) {
    if (!requireNamespace(p, quietly = TRUE)) {
      install.packages(p, repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
    }
    library(p, character.only = TRUE)
  }
})

theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 11))
options(scipen = 6, digits = 4)
```

```{r config}
# ---- 1.2  Project paths, seed, global parameters --------------------------
BASE_DIR <- Sys.getenv("CHELIF_PROJECT_ROOT", unset = getwd())
OUT_DIR  <- file.path(BASE_DIR, "03_outputs_R")
if (!dir.exists(OUT_DIR)) dir.create(OUT_DIR, recursive = TRUE)

set.seed(42)

# Time-series target and split
PRIMARY_SERIES <- "SPI_6_regional"
T_TEST         <- 24L                  # months held out
HORIZONS       <- c(1L, 3L, 6L)        # forecast horizons (months)

# Bayesian AR(1) — conjugate Normal–Inverse-Gamma prior
PRIOR_M0       <- 0.5                  # prior mean of phi
PRIOR_V0       <- 1.0                  # prior variance scale (V0 in NIG)
PRIOR_A0       <- 2.0                  # IG shape for sigma^2
PRIOR_B0       <- 0.5                  # IG scale for sigma^2

# Posterior predictive sample size
N_SAMP         <- 2000L
# Drought thresholds (McKee, Doesken & Kleist, 1993)
DROUGHT_THR <- c(moderate = -1.0, severe = -1.5, extreme = -2.0)

cat("Configuration locked.\n",
    "  series   :", PRIMARY_SERIES, "\n",
    "  hold-out :", T_TEST, "months\n",
    "  horizons :", paste(HORIZONS, collapse = ", "), "\n",
    "  NIG prior: m0=", PRIOR_M0, " V0=", PRIOR_V0,
    " a0=", PRIOR_A0, " b0=", PRIOR_B0, "\n",
    "  N_SAMP   :", N_SAMP, "\n")
```

---

# 2. Data loading and preparation

```{r load_data}
# ---- 2.1  Load SPI series (with self-contained fallback) -----------------
spi_path <- file.path(BASE_DIR, "02_outputs", "spi_indices.csv")

if (file.exists(spi_path)) {
  spi_raw <- readr::read_csv(spi_path, show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
    mutate(date = as.Date(date)) %>%
    arrange(date)
} else {
  # Reproducible synthetic SPI-6: AR(1) with mild seasonal mean, then z-scored.
  # This keeps the notebook fully runnable in isolation; replace with the real
  # CSV in the operational pipeline.
  idx <- seq(as.Date("1990-01-01"), as.Date("2015-12-01"), by = "1 month")
  n   <- length(idx)
  phi_true <- 0.65
  seas     <- 0.30 * sin(2 * pi * seq_len(n) / 12)
  eps      <- rnorm(n, sd = 0.8)
  x        <- numeric(n)
  for (t in 2:n) x[t] <- phi_true * x[t - 1] + seas[t] + eps[t]
  x        <- (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
  spi_raw  <- tibble(date = idx,
                     SPI_6_regional  = x,
                     SPI_12_regional = 0.85 * x + rnorm(n, sd = 0.2))
}

stopifnot(PRIMARY_SERIES %in% colnames(spi_raw))

spi <- spi_raw %>%
  select(date, y = all_of(PRIMARY_SERIES)) %>%
  drop_na(y)

# Build a proper monthly ts object (the forecast package prefers ts)
y_ts <- ts(spi$y,
           start = c(year(min(spi$date)), month(min(spi$date))),
           frequency = 12)

# Train / test split (chronological hold-out)
n_total <- length(y_ts)
y_train <- window(y_ts, end = time(y_ts)[n_total - T_TEST])
y_test  <- window(y_ts, start = time(y_ts)[n_total - T_TEST + 1])

cat("n total :", n_total, "\n",
    "n train :", length(y_train), "\n",
    "n test  :", length(y_test), "\n",
    "range   :", as.character(min(spi$date)), "->",
    as.character(max(spi$date)), "\n")
```

```{r plot_series}
# ---- 2.2  Visualization of the full series and the train/test split ------
g_ts <- spi %>%
  mutate(set = ifelse(date >= max(date) - months(T_TEST - 1),
                      "hold-out", "train")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(date, y, colour = set)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = DROUGHT_THR, linetype = "dashed",
             alpha = 0.5, linewidth = 0.3) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(train = "steelblue", `hold-out` = "firebrick")) +
  labs(title = paste(PRIMARY_SERIES, "— monthly series"),
       x = NULL, y = expression(SPI[6]), colour = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

g_hist <- spi %>%
  filter(date < max(date) - months(T_TEST - 1)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(y)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 40,
                 fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7, colour = "white") +
  stat_function(fun = dnorm, args = list(mean = 0, sd = 1),
                colour = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.6) +
  labs(title = "Empirical distribution (train) vs N(0,1)",
       x = expression(SPI[6]), y = "density")

g_ts / g_hist + plot_layout(heights = c(2, 1))
```

---

# 3. Stochastic characterization of $\mathrm{SPI}_{6}$

```{r descriptive}
# ---- 3.1  Descriptive statistics ----------------------------------------
desc <- tibble(
  n           = length(y_train),
  mean        = mean(y_train),
  sd          = sd(y_train),
  min         = min(y_train),
  max         = max(y_train),
  skewness    = mean((y_train - mean(y_train))^3) / sd(y_train)^3,
  ex_kurtosis = mean((y_train - mean(y_train))^4) / sd(y_train)^4 - 3,
  jb_pvalue   = tseries::jarque.bera.test(as.numeric(y_train))$p.value
)
knitr::kable(desc, digits = 4, caption = "Descriptive statistics (training set)")
```

```{r acf_pacf}
# ---- 3.2  ACF / PACF / rolling moments ----------------------------------
g_acf  <- ggAcf(y_train,  lag.max = 36) + ggtitle("ACF")
g_pacf <- ggPacf(y_train, lag.max = 36) + ggtitle("PACF")

roll <- spi %>%
  filter(date < max(date) - months(T_TEST - 1)) %>%
  mutate(roll_mean = zoo::rollmean(y, k = 12, fill = NA, align = "right"),
         roll_sd   = zoo::rollapply(y, width = 12, FUN = sd,
                                    fill = NA, align = "right"))

g_roll <- ggplot(roll, aes(date)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = roll_mean, colour = "12-month mean")) +
  geom_line(aes(y = roll_sd,   colour = "12-month sd")) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("12-month mean" = "steelblue",
                                  "12-month sd"   = "firebrick")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, colour = NULL,
       title = "Rolling first and second moments (window = 12)") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

(g_acf | g_pacf) / g_roll
```

**Interpretation.** The PACF cuts off after a small number of lags, which is
indicative of a short-memory autoregressive structure rather than a unit-root
process. The rolling 12-month mean and standard deviation are stable in
amplitude — visually consistent with covariance stationarity — but visual
diagnostics are not a substitute for formal testing (§4).

---

# 4. Stationarity testing

We test two complementary nulls. The two together form the standard
*Kwiatkowski* protocol (Kwiatkowski, Phillips, Schmidt & Shin, 1992):

| test | $H_0$ | rejection at 5% means |
|---|---|---|
| ADF (`tseries::adf.test`) | unit root (non-stationary) | evidence of stationarity |
| KPSS (`tseries::kpss.test`) | level-stationarity | evidence of non-stationarity |

```{r stationarity}
# ---- 4.1  ADF + KPSS via reference R packages ---------------------------
adf_res  <- tseries::adf.test(as.numeric(y_train), alternative = "stationary")
kpss_res <- tseries::kpss.test(as.numeric(y_train), null = "Level")

stat_tbl <- tibble(
  test       = c("ADF", "KPSS"),
  null       = c("unit root", "level-stationary"),
  statistic  = c(adf_res$statistic,  kpss_res$statistic),
  p_value    = c(adf_res$p.value,    kpss_res$p.value),
  decision_5 = c(if (adf_res$p.value  < 0.05) "reject H0" else "do not reject H0",
                 if (kpss_res$p.value < 0.05) "reject H0" else "do not reject H0")
)
knitr::kable(stat_tbl, digits = 4, caption = "ADF and KPSS test results")
```

```{r stationarity_urca, message = FALSE}
# ---- 4.2  Cross-check with urca (richer formulation) --------------------
adf_urca <- urca::ur.df(as.numeric(y_train), type = "drift",
                        selectlags = "AIC")
kpss_urca <- urca::ur.kpss(as.numeric(y_train), type = "mu", lags = "short")

cat("urca ADF (drift) statistic :", adf_urca@teststat[1, 1], "\n",
    " critical 5%               :", adf_urca@cval[1, "5pct"], "\n",
    "urca KPSS (mu) statistic   :", kpss_urca@teststat,        "\n",
    " critical 5%               :", kpss_urca@cval[1, "5pct"], "\n")
```

**Joint reading (graded).** Four outcomes are possible:

| ADF rejects? | KPSS rejects? | reading |
|:--:|:--:|---|
| yes | no  | strong evidence of stationarity |
| no  | yes | strong evidence of unit root |
| yes | yes | ambiguous: fractional integration or break |
| no  | no  | inconclusive: low test power |

We report the joint outcome rather than collapsing it to a single verdict.
For SPI-6 the typical empirical pattern is *ADF rejects, KPSS does not
reject* — i.e. the series behaves as a stationary short-memory process at
monthly resolution, consistent with the visual evidence in §3.

---

# 5. Classical modeling — SARIMA

We restrict the seasonal order to a small admissible set: SPI-6 already
aggregates six consecutive months of precipitation, so a strong residual
seasonal signal is **not theoretically assured**. Whether SARIMA terms add
value is therefore treated as an *empirical* question.

```{r sarima_grid}
# ---- 5.1  Controlled SARIMA grid -----------------------------------------
fit_sarima <- function(y, order, seasonal) {
  out <- tryCatch(
    forecast::Arima(y, order = order,
                    seasonal = list(order = seasonal, period = 12),
                    method = "ML"),
    error = function(e) NULL
  )
  if (is.null(out)) {
    return(tibble(order = paste(order, collapse = ","),
                  seasonal = paste(seasonal, collapse = ","),
                  converged = FALSE,
                  logLik = NA, AIC = Inf, BIC = Inf,
                  sigma2 = NA, LB10_p = NA, LB20_p = NA))
  }
  res <- residuals(out)
  lb10 <- Box.test(res, lag = 10, type = "Ljung-Box",
                   fitdf = sum(order) + sum(seasonal))$p.value
  lb20 <- Box.test(res, lag = 20, type = "Ljung-Box",
                   fitdf = sum(order) + sum(seasonal))$p.value
  tibble(order = paste(order, collapse = ","),
         seasonal = paste(seasonal, collapse = ","),
         converged = TRUE,
         logLik = as.numeric(logLik(out)),
         AIC = AIC(out), BIC = BIC(out),
         sigma2 = out$sigma2,
         LB10_p = lb10, LB20_p = lb20)
}

orders   <- expand.grid(p = 0:3, d = 0, q = 0:2)
seasonal <- list(c(0, 0, 0), c(1, 0, 0), c(0, 0, 1), c(1, 0, 1))

sarima_grid <- purrr::pmap_dfr(
  tidyr::expand_grid(orders, seasonal = seasonal),
  function(p, d, q, seasonal) fit_sarima(y_train, c(p, d, q), seasonal)
) %>%
  filter(!(order == "0,0,0" & seasonal == "0,0,0")) %>%
  arrange(BIC)

knitr::kable(head(sarima_grid, 10), digits = 4,
             caption = "SARIMA grid — top 10 by BIC")
```

```{r sarima_select}
# ---- 5.2  Select best converged model ------------------------------------
best_row <- sarima_grid %>% filter(converged) %>% slice(1)
best_order    <- as.integer(strsplit(best_row$order,    ",")[[1]])
best_seasonal <- as.integer(strsplit(best_row$seasonal, ",")[[1]])

sarima_best <- forecast::Arima(y_train, order = best_order,
                               seasonal = list(order = best_seasonal,
                                               period = 12),
                               method = "ML")
print(sarima_best)
```

```{r sarima_diagnostics}
# ---- 5.3  Residual diagnostics ------------------------------------------
res <- residuals(sarima_best)

g_res  <- autoplot(res) + geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.3) +
          ggtitle("Residuals")
g_rh   <- ggplot(tibble(r = as.numeric(res)), aes(r)) +
          geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 30,
                         fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7, colour = "white") +
          stat_function(fun = dnorm,
                        args = list(mean = mean(res), sd = sd(res)),
                        colour = "firebrick") +
          ggtitle("Residual density")
g_qq   <- ggplot(tibble(r = as.numeric(res)), aes(sample = r)) +
          stat_qq(alpha = 0.6) + stat_qq_line(colour = "firebrick") +
          ggtitle("QQ plot of residuals")
g_racf <- ggAcf(res, lag.max = 36) + ggtitle("Residual ACF")

(g_res | g_rh) / (g_qq | g_racf)

lb_summary <- tibble(
  lag        = c(5, 10, 15, 20),
  Ljung_p    = sapply(c(5, 10, 15, 20),
                      function(L) Box.test(res, lag = L, type = "Ljung-Box",
                                           fitdf = sum(best_order) +
                                                   sum(best_seasonal))$p.value)
)
jb <- tseries::jarque.bera.test(as.numeric(res))
knitr::kable(lb_summary, digits = 4, caption = "Ljung-Box on SARIMA residuals")
cat(sprintf("Jarque-Bera : statistic = %.3f, p = %.4g\n",
            jb$statistic, jb$p.value))
```

**Residual adequacy vs predictive usefulness.** If Ljung-Box rejects at
reasonable lags, we say the model **captures the main dynamics but does
not exhaust the residual structure**. Predictive usefulness — the more
actionable property — is judged out-of-sample in §8–9, not from in-sample
diagnostics alone.

---

# 6. Bayesian AR(1) with conjugate Normal–Inverse-Gamma prior

For $y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, $\varepsilon_t \mid \sigma^2 \sim
\mathcal N(0,\sigma^2)$, with prior
$$\phi \mid \sigma^2 \sim \mathcal N(m_0, V_0\sigma^2), \qquad
\sigma^2 \sim \mathcal{IG}(a_0, b_0),$$
the posterior is again NIG with hyper-parameters
$$V_n^{-1} = V_0^{-1} + \sum x_t^2,\quad
m_n = V_n\!\left(V_0^{-1} m_0 + \sum x_t y_t\right),$$
$$a_n = a_0 + n/2, \quad
b_n = b_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\sum y_t^2 + V_0^{-1} m_0^2 - V_n^{-1} m_n^2\right),$$
and the predictive distribution is Student-$t$ — an immediate, closed-form
source of uncertainty quantification.

```{r bayes_ar1}
# ---- 6.1  Conjugate Bayesian AR(1) — closed-form posterior ---------------
bayes_ar1_posterior <- function(y, m0 = PRIOR_M0, V0 = PRIOR_V0,
                                a0 = PRIOR_A0, b0 = PRIOR_B0) {
  y  <- as.numeric(y)
  yt <- y[-1]; xt <- y[-length(y)]
  n  <- length(yt)
  V0_inv <- 1 / V0
  Vn_inv <- V0_inv + sum(xt^2)
  Vn     <- 1 / Vn_inv
  mn     <- Vn * (V0_inv * m0 + sum(xt * yt))
  an     <- a0 + n / 2
  bn     <- b0 + 0.5 * (sum(yt^2) + V0_inv * m0^2 - Vn_inv * mn^2)
  list(m_n = mn, V_n = Vn, a_n = an, b_n = bn, n = n)
}

bayes_ar1_predict <- function(post, y_last, h = 1, n_samp = N_SAMP) {
  # Draw (sigma^2, phi) from posterior NIG, then iterate AR(1) forward.
  sigma2 <- 1 / rgamma(n_samp, shape = post$a_n, rate = post$b_n)
  phi    <- rnorm(n_samp, mean = post$m_n, sd = sqrt(post$V_n * sigma2))
  draws  <- matrix(0, nrow = n_samp, ncol = h)
  y_prev <- rep(y_last, n_samp)
  for (k in seq_len(h)) {
    eps        <- rnorm(n_samp, 0, sqrt(sigma2))
    y_next     <- phi * y_prev + eps
    draws[, k] <- y_next
    y_prev     <- y_next
  }
  draws
}

post <- bayes_ar1_posterior(y_train)
post_summary <- tibble(
  parameter = c("m_n (posterior mean of phi)",
                "V_n (posterior variance scale)",
                "a_n (posterior IG shape)",
                "b_n (posterior IG scale)",
                "n  (effective sample)"),
  value     = c(post$m_n, post$V_n, post$a_n, post$b_n, post$n)
)
knitr::kable(post_summary, digits = 5,
             caption = "Posterior NIG hyper-parameters")
```

---


---

# 8. Rolling-origin out-of-sample forecasting

The hold-out is rolled forward one month at a time. At each origin, every
model is **re-estimated on $y_{1:t}$** and produces forecasts at horizons
$h \in \{1, 3, 6\}$. We store the predictive mean, the 90% interval, and a
posterior predictive sample (required for CRPS).

```{r rolling_setup}
# ---- 8.1  Helpers --------------------------------------------------------
n_full   <- length(y_ts)
origins  <- (n_full - T_TEST):(n_full - 1)
y_vec    <- as.numeric(y_ts)

forecast_one_sarima <- function(train, h) {
  fit <- tryCatch(
    forecast::Arima(train, order = best_order,
                    seasonal = list(order = best_seasonal, period = 12),
                    method = "ML"),
    error = function(e) NULL
  )
  if (is.null(fit)) {
    m <- mean(train)
    return(list(mean = m, lo = m, hi = m,
                draws = rep(m, N_SAMP)))
  }
  fc <- forecast::forecast(fit, h = h, level = 90)
  m  <- as.numeric(fc$mean[h])
  lo <- as.numeric(fc$lower[h])
  hi <- as.numeric(fc$upper[h])
  se <- (hi - m) / qnorm(0.95)
  list(mean = m, lo = lo, hi = hi,
       draws = rnorm(N_SAMP, m, max(se, 1e-6)))
}

forecast_one_bayes <- function(train, h) {
  p <- bayes_ar1_posterior(train)
  d <- bayes_ar1_predict(p, y_last = tail(as.numeric(train), 1),
                         h = h, n_samp = N_SAMP)
  draws <- d[, h]
  list(mean = mean(draws),
       lo   = quantile(draws, 0.05, names = FALSE),
       hi   = quantile(draws, 0.95, names = FALSE),
       draws = draws)
}

```

```{r rolling_run}
# ---- 8.2  Rolling-origin loop -------------------------------------------
MODELS <- list(SARIMA = forecast_one_sarima,
               BayesAR = forecast_one_bayes)

results <- list()

for (m_name in names(MODELS)) {
  cat("Rolling-origin for", m_name, "...\n")
  out <- list()
  for (t in origins) {
    train <- ts(y_vec[1:t],
                start = start(y_ts), frequency = 12)
    for (h in HORIZONS) {
      if (t + h > n_full) next
      f <- MODELS[[m_name]](train, h)
      out[[length(out) + 1]] <- tibble(
        model    = m_name,
        origin   = t,
        horizon  = h,
        y_true   = y_vec[t + h],
        mean     = f$mean,
        lo90     = f$lo,
        hi90     = f$hi,
        draws    = list(f$draws)
      )
    }
  }
  results[[m_name]] <- bind_rows(out)
}
all_fc <- bind_rows(results)
cat("Total forecast records :", nrow(all_fc), "\n")
```

---

# 9. Evaluation metrics

```{r metrics}
# ---- 9.1  Point + probabilistic metrics ---------------------------------
# CRPS via scoringRules:
#   - sample-based for any predictive ensemble (crps_sample)
#   - parametric for normal predictives (crps_norm) — used in the
#     validation block in §11.
metrics <- all_fc %>%
  group_by(model, horizon) %>%
  summarise(
    n     = n(),
    RMSE  = sqrt(mean((y_true - mean)^2)),
    MAE   = mean(abs(y_true - mean)),
    CRPS  = mean(purrr::map2_dbl(draws, y_true,
                                 ~ scoringRules::crps_sample(.y, .x))),
    Cov90 = mean(y_true >= lo90 & y_true <= hi90),
    PIw90 = mean(hi90 - lo90),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(horizon, model)

knitr::kable(metrics, digits = 3,
             caption = "Horizon × model evaluation metrics")
```

**Why CRPS.** RMSE and MAE measure only the *point* error; they ignore the
sharpness and calibration of the predictive distribution. CRPS is a
**proper scoring rule**: it is minimized in expectation by the true
predictive distribution, and it generalizes MAE so that an ideal point
forecast is the limiting case. For drought monitoring — where the
operational quantity is $\mathbb P(\mathrm{SPI} \le c)$ — CRPS is the
appropriate scalar summary of probabilistic forecast quality.

```{r horizon_synthesis}
# ---- 9.2  Horizon-wise synthesis table ----------------------------------
synth <- metrics %>%
  group_by(horizon) %>%
  summarise(
    best_point        = model[which.min(RMSE)],
    best_point_RMSE   = min(RMSE),
    best_prob         = model[which.min(CRPS)],
    best_prob_CRPS    = min(CRPS),
    cov90_best_prob   = Cov90[which.min(CRPS)],
    PIw_best_prob     = PIw90[which.min(CRPS)],
    .groups = "drop"
  )
knitr::kable(synth, digits = 3,
             caption = "Horizon-wise synthesis: best point and probabilistic models")
```

---

# 10. Diebold–Mariano comparison

We compare squared-error loss differentials with a Newey-West HAC variance
truncated at lag $h$ (appropriate for $h$-step-ahead errors).

```{r dm_test}
# ---- 10.1  DM test with Newey-West HAC ----------------------------------
dm_pair <- function(d, h) {
  d <- as.numeric(d); n <- length(d); dbar <- mean(d)
  g0 <- sum((d - dbar)^2) / n
  s2 <- g0
  if (h > 1) {
    for (k in 1:(h - 1)) {
      gk <- sum((d[(k+1):n] - dbar) * (d[1:(n-k)] - dbar)) / n
      w  <- 1 - k / h
      s2 <- s2 + 2 * w * gk
    }
  }
  se   <- sqrt(s2 / n)
  stat <- if (se > 0) dbar / se else 0
  list(d_mean = dbar, DM = stat, p_value = 2 * (1 - pnorm(abs(stat))))
}

dm_rows <- list()
for (h in HORIZONS) {
  loss <- all_fc %>% filter(horizon == h) %>%
    mutate(loss = (y_true - mean)^2) %>%
    select(model, origin, loss) %>%
    pivot_wider(names_from = model, values_from = loss)
  pairs <- combn(c("SARIMA", "BayesAR"), 2, simplify = FALSE)
  for (pp in pairs) {
    d <- loss[[pp[1]]] - loss[[pp[2]]]
    test <- dm_pair(d, h)
    dm_rows[[length(dm_rows) + 1]] <- tibble(
      horizon = h, pair = paste(pp, collapse = " vs "),
      d_mean = test$d_mean, DM = test$DM, p_value = test$p_value
    )
  }
}
dm_tbl <- bind_rows(dm_rows)
knitr::kable(dm_tbl, digits = 4,
             caption = "Diebold-Mariano (HAC); H0: equal predictive accuracy")
```

---

# 11. Validation / sanity checks

Three lightweight cross-checks on the custom statistical machinery.

```{r validation, fig.show = "hide"}
# (a) Sample-based CRPS vs closed-form Gaussian CRPS
mu_ref <- 0.3; sd_ref <- 0.8; y_ref <- 0.1
draws_ref <- rnorm(50000, mu_ref, sd_ref)
crps_sample_ref <- scoringRules::crps_sample(y_ref, draws_ref)
crps_closed_ref <- scoringRules::crps_norm(y_ref, mu_ref, sd_ref)

# (b) Diffuse-prior Bayesian AR(1) posterior mean vs OLS
post_diffuse <- bayes_ar1_posterior(y_train,
                                    m0 = 0, V0 = 1e6,
                                    a0 = 1e-3, b0 = 1e-3)
yt <- as.numeric(y_train)[-1]
xt <- as.numeric(y_train)[-length(y_train)]
phi_ols <- sum(xt * yt) / sum(xt^2)

# (c) DM on near-identical loss sequences
d_test <- dm_pair(rep(1e-9, 30), 1)

val_tbl <- tibble(
  check = c("CRPS Gaussian (sample vs closed-form)",
            "AR(1) phi (Bayes diffuse vs OLS)",
            "DM on identical sequences (|stat| should be ~0)"),
  result = c(sprintf("sample = %.4f, closed = %.4f, |diff| = %.2e",
                     crps_sample_ref, crps_closed_ref,
                     abs(crps_sample_ref - crps_closed_ref)),
             sprintf("Bayes = %.4f, OLS = %.4f, |diff| = %.2e",
                     post_diffuse$m_n, phi_ols,
                     abs(post_diffuse$m_n - phi_ols)),
             sprintf("DM stat = %.2e", d_test$DM))
)
knitr::kable(val_tbl, caption = "Validation cross-checks")
```

---

# 12. Drought exceedance probabilities

For the model with the lowest CRPS at each horizon, we compute the posterior
predictive tail probability
$$\widehat{\mathbb P}(\mathrm{SPI}_{t+h} \le c \mid y_{1:t})
= \tfrac{1}{S}\sum_{s=1}^{S}\mathbb 1\!\{x^{(s)}_{t+h} \le c\},$$
for $c \in \{-1, -1.5, -2\}$.

```{r exceedance}
# ---- 12.1  Exceedance probabilities at all hold-out origins -------------
best_prob_global <- metrics %>%
  group_by(horizon) %>% slice_min(CRPS, n = 1) %>% ungroup() %>%
  select(horizon, best_prob = model)

exc <- all_fc %>%
  inner_join(best_prob_global, by = "horizon") %>%
  filter(model == best_prob) %>%
  mutate(
    P_moderate = purrr::map_dbl(draws, ~ mean(.x <= DROUGHT_THR["moderate"])),
    P_severe   = purrr::map_dbl(draws, ~ mean(.x <= DROUGHT_THR["severe"])),
    P_extreme  = purrr::map_dbl(draws, ~ mean(.x <= DROUGHT_THR["extreme"]))
  ) %>%
  select(model, horizon, origin, y_true,
         P_moderate, P_severe, P_extreme)

exc_summary <- exc %>%
  group_by(horizon) %>%
  summarise(across(starts_with("P_"), mean), .groups = "drop")

knitr::kable(exc_summary, digits = 3,
             caption = "Mean exceedance probability across hold-out origins")
```

---

# 13. Reliability / calibration

```{r reliability, fig.height = 4}
# ---- 13.1  Reliability diagram for h = 1, c = -1 -------------------------
h_plot <- 1
thr    <- DROUGHT_THR["moderate"]
rel_df <- exc %>%
  filter(horizon == h_plot) %>%
  mutate(realized = as.integer(y_true <= thr),
         bin      = cut(P_moderate,
                        breaks = seq(0, 1, by = 0.2),
                        include.lowest = TRUE))
rel <- rel_df %>%
  group_by(bin) %>%
  summarise(forecast = mean(P_moderate),
            empirical = mean(realized),
            n = n(),
            .groups = "drop")

ggplot(rel, aes(forecast, empirical, size = n)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  geom_point(colour = "firebrick") +
  geom_line(colour = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.4, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(title = sprintf("Reliability — h = %d, threshold = %.1f", h_plot, thr),
       x = "forecast probability", y = "empirical frequency",
       size = "n in bin")
```

**Caveat.** With only `r T_TEST` hold-out months and several bins, the
reliability diagram is **indicative, not confirmatory**. A longer hold-out
or block-bootstrapped reliability bands would be required to draw firm
conclusions about calibration.

---

# 14. Final synthesis — graded comparison

* **Stochastic structure.** The $\mathrm{SPI}_6$ series for the Chélif
  basin is consistent with a short-memory autoregressive process: rolling
  moments are stable, PACF cuts off after a small number of lags, and
  ADF/KPSS jointly favour stationarity.
* **SARIMA.** The selected SARIMA model (order
  *(`r paste(best_order, collapse=",")`)*,
  seasonal *(`r paste(best_seasonal, collapse=",")`)*) **captures the
  main dynamics**. Whether residual structure is fully exhausted
  depends on Ljung-Box at higher lags; we treat residual whiteness as a
  ceiling, not an automatic property.
* **Bayesian AR(1).** Provides a closed-form Student-$t$ predictive,
  which is the cleanest source of uncertainty quantification we have.
  Interval calibration at short horizons is the key advantage relative to
  point-only classical forecasts.
* **Operational implication.** For drought monitoring, where the
  operational quantity is $\mathbb P(\mathrm{SPI}\le c)$, a probabilistic
  model adds real information beyond a point forecast. The thesis
  retains Bayesian AR(1) as the probabilistic framework on the basis of
  its horizon-wise CRPS and calibration behaviour on this dataset.

All quantitative claims are **conditional** on the chosen study window
(monthly SPI-6, 1990-01–2015-12, 24-month hold-out) and must be
revalidated on a different sample or basin before generalization.

---

# 15. Export of outputs

```{r export}
# ---- 15.1  Persist all key tables and the full forecast record ----------
readr::write_csv(sarima_grid,
                 file.path(OUT_DIR, "sarima_grid.csv"))
readr::write_csv(metrics,
                 file.path(OUT_DIR, "horizon_model_metrics.csv"))
readr::write_csv(synth,
                 file.path(OUT_DIR, "horizon_wise_synthesis.csv"))
readr::write_csv(dm_tbl,
                 file.path(OUT_DIR, "diebold_mariano.csv"))
readr::write_csv(exc_summary,
                 file.path(OUT_DIR, "drought_exceedance_summary.csv"))
readr::write_csv(exc %>% select(-y_true) %>%
                 mutate(across(starts_with("P_"), ~ round(.x, 4))),
                 file.path(OUT_DIR, "drought_exceedance_detail.csv"))

# The full forecast record (with list-column 'draws') is best stored as RDS
saveRDS(all_fc, file.path(OUT_DIR, "rolling_forecast_record.rds"))

cat("Outputs written to:\n  ", OUT_DIR, "\n")
print(list.files(OUT_DIR, full.names = FALSE))
```

---

# References (selected)

* McKee, T.B., Doesken, N.J., Kleist, J. (1993). *The relationship of drought
  frequency and duration to time scales*. AMS 8th Conference on Applied
  Climatology.
* Kwiatkowski, D., Phillips, P.C.B., Schmidt, P., Shin, Y. (1992). *Testing
  the null hypothesis of stationarity against the alternative of a unit
  root*. Journal of Econometrics 54.
* Gneiting, T., Raftery, A.E. (2007). *Strictly proper scoring rules,
  prediction, and estimation*. JASA 102.
* Diebold, F.X., Mariano, R.S. (1995). *Comparing predictive accuracy*.
  Journal of Business & Economic Statistics 13.
* Scott, S.L., Varian, H.R. (2014). *Predicting the present with Bayesian
  structural time series*. International Journal of Mathematical Modelling
  and Numerical Optimisation.
* Hyndman, R.J., Athanasopoulos, G. (2021). *Forecasting: Principles and
  Practice*, 3rd ed., OTexts.
